text: The surgeon carefully dissects adhesions to expose the inferior edge of the right lobe of the liver. The hepatic flexure of the colon is moved aside to facilitate safe lateral to medial dissection, aiming to identify the gallbladder at the fundus or lateral aspect of the body.

The surgeon demonstrates a top-down cholecystectomy approach, carefully dissecting directly on the gallbladder wall to avoid the liver parenchyma. The body of the gallbladder is visibly freed from the liver fossa, with the cholecystostomy tube still in place.

The surgeon retracts the liver to expose the cystic structures. Additional ports are used for better access. The gallbladder is transected at the neck, revealing an impacted stone, which is removed and set aside. After cleaning out residual debris, the interior view shows the infundibulum narrowing into the cystic duct, and surrounding tissue is dissected off the cystic duct.

The surgeon uses lateral retraction and careful dissection to avoid injury to portal structures near the gallbladder, particularly in cases with a short cystic duct. A small defect is created in the cystic duct, which is then secured with an endo-loop. The cystic artery is divided at its terminal branches during the top-down cholecystectomy. Once the duct is secured, the remaining gallbladder is resected, and gallbladder pieces and stones are collected.
